message:
perf(tools/resources): offload reservation_compute_pattern_activity to
spawn_blocking and keep the cache correct under the new await boundary

`file_reservations` computes per-pattern activity (filesystem mtime
scan + git log for each held reservation) inside its result-building
loop. The previous code used
`pattern_activity_cache.entry(...).or_insert_with(|closure|)` to
memoize per-`path_pattern`, but:

1. `reservation_compute_pattern_activity` does real blocking I/O (fs
   metadata walk + subprocess git log). Calling it on the async
   dispatcher thread pins the runtime worker for the duration of the
   scan — noticeable on repos with deep workspaces or many holders.
2. `or_insert_with` can only take a synchronous closure, so there was
   no way to introduce an `.await` boundary without restructuring.

Switch to an explicit `if let Some(val) = cache.get(...) {...} else`
shape:

* On hit: clone and use the cached `PatternActivity` (unchanged).
* On miss: clone the three `Path` inputs (`workspace`, `repo_root`,
  `workspace_rel`) into owned `PathBuf`s (because the closure moves
  into `spawn_blocking`), call
  `asupersync::runtime::spawn_blocking` to run
  `reservation_compute_pattern_activity` on the blocking pool,
  `.await` the result, and insert it into the cache before using it.

The `.as_deref()` on the cloned `PathBuf`s preserves the exact
`Option<&Path>` signature of the underlying compute function, so the
activity result is byte-for-byte identical — no behaviour change, just
a different execution thread for the I/O-heavy portion and an explicit
await point that lets other file-reservation rows proceed while one
pattern's scan is in flight. Downstream `recent_fs` / `recent_git`
checks still consume the same `PatternActivity` shape. The cache is
still hit on subsequent rows that share `row.path_pattern`, so the
number of scans is still O(unique patterns) rather than O(rows).
